1.blurb: While the Harappans were running the largest urban civilization on earth, Mesopotamia was a fraction of the size — Sargon of Akkad had just stitched its warring Sumerian city-states into the ancient world's first empire, and the whole thing would have fit inside a single Harappan province. Mesopotamia gets all the press because its kings left readable texts; the Indus was bigger, but silent.

Cuneiform was born at Uruk around 3200 BCE as accountants needed a way to track temple grain, then evolved from pictographs into the wedge-shaped script pressed into wet clay by the hundreds of thousands. It is the reason we know the names of Mesopotamian kings, priests, and merchants — and the reason the Harappan silence feels so loud.

Sargon of Akkad (ruling roughly 2334–2279 BCE) built the world's first empire by conquering the Sumerian city-states one by one and installing his daughter Enheduanna as high priestess of the moon god. His inscriptions brag about ships from Meluhha — almost certainly the Indus — docking at his capital. He is the first named empire-builder in human history.

Hammurabi of Babylon (reigning ~1792–1750 BCE, right as the Harappan cities were emptying out) left an eight-foot diorite pillar carved with 282 laws — the most famous legal code in the ancient world — and his personal letters survive in the thousands. The Harappans had maybe ten times his population and left not a single readable sentence.

2.blurb: When Mehrgarh's first farmers were drilling teeth in 7000 BCE, Mesopotamia was still a patchwork of early Neolithic villages — Hassuna and Samarra communities making painted pottery, experimenting with irrigation, and slowly figuring out how to farm a land that only got rain in the north. The cities were still four thousand years away.

At the exact moment Mehrgarh's farmers were domesticating wheat and barley, villagers in China's Yellow River valley were independently domesticating foxtail and broomcorn millet — the two tough, drought-resistant grains that would feed northern China for the next seven thousand years. Three farming revolutions, three grain packages, all starting around 7000 BCE with no contact between them.

Mesopotamia was smelting copper by around 5000 BCE too, in the same Ubaid-period villages that would eventually grow into the first cities — the two regions hit the metal age on roughly the same schedule, probably because copper-working know-how was leaking slowly eastward out of the Iranian plateau into both worlds at once.

4.blurb: Mesopotamia's Early Dynastic period (~2900–2350 BCE) was the era of warring city-states — Uruk, Ur, Lagash, Umma — each ruled by a lugal (big man) fighting his neighbors over canals and prestige. This is the world of Gilgamesh, the Sumerian King List, and the Royal Tombs of Ur. Compared to the Harappan grid cities appearing at the same moment, it was smaller, messier, and a lot more violent.

4.matchText: Liangzhu city of the Yangtze delta
4.blurb: Liangzhu, near modern Hangzhou, was running a 100-square-kilometer hydraulic flood-control network and an industrial-scale jade industry by 3000 BCE — the first state-level society anywhere in East Asia, and a near-exact contemporary of the earliest Mature Harappan cities. Its elite were buried with hundreds of carved jade cong tubes and bi discs; nobody knew it existed until the 1930s.

While the Harappans were laying out grid cities with covered drains, China's Yellow River plain was filling up with Longshan walled towns — hundreds of competing rammed-earth-walled settlements run by warlord chiefs who left elite jade-rich burials and foundation sacrifices under the walls. No grid plans, no shared weights, just a patchwork of proto-states gearing up for the first dynasty.

4.matchText: fourth cradle was active at the same moment
4.blurb: A fifth Bronze Age cradle was not far behind. Around 2400 BCE, in Upper Nubia just south of the Third Cataract, the city of Kerma began rising with its monumental Western Deffufa temple — the first urbanized society in Africa outside the Egyptian delta. Kerma is the last of the great Bronze Age cradles to cross the urban threshold, arriving roughly two centuries after Mohenjo-daro.

5.matchText: Mesopotamian maritime trade was well-established by this date
5.blurb: By the Early Dynastic period, Sumerian cities like Ur and Lagash were already running boats down the Persian Gulf to trade with Dilmun (Bahrain) and Magan (Oman). What they didn't have was anything like Lothal's engineered tidal dock — their 'ports' were basically riverbanks with mooring posts. The Harappans were the ones who turned a beach landing into civil engineering.

The Iranian-related farming lineage in the Rakhigarhi DNA is a signature of the same western-Iranian Neolithic populations who founded Susa around 4200 BCE — one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities on earth. The Indus and Elam weren't just trading partners; they shared deep genetic ancestry in the early farmers of the Zagros foothills, and the first Harappan farmers walked out of the same highland prehistoric world that gave Elam its own origin story.

6.blurb: The Royal Tombs of Ur (excavated by Leonard Woolley in the 1920s) held Queen Puabi, dozens of sacrificed attendants laid out in rows, golden helmets, lapis lazuli harps, and bull-headed lyres. They are the single most spectacular elite burial complex of the early Bronze Age — and everything about them (the names, the hierarchy, the sacrifices, the gold) is precisely what the Harappan cemeteries refuse to give us.

At the exact moment the Indus was (apparently) running cities without kings, China's Longshan horizon was doing the opposite — hardening into competing warlord proto-states with rammed-earth walls, elite jade burials, oracle-bone divination, and human sacrifices buried in wall foundations. The Yellow River plain was sprinting toward dynastic kingship while the Indus seems to have been actively avoiding it.

6.matchText: only major ancient civilization from which we cannot name a single historical individual
6.blurb: The Indus has one close companion in this silence: the Kerma kingdom of Upper Nubia (~2500–1500 BCE). Kerma ran a large urban state for a thousand years, built monumental mud-brick deffufa temples, buried its kings in tumuli with hundreds of sacrificed retainers, and left no writing of its own that we can read. We know Kerma's kings only archaeologically, by their tombs. The two great 'missing voices' of the Bronze Age are missing in different ways — Indus had a script that hasn't been deciphered, Kerma had no script at all — but both force the same question about how a complex state governs itself without the tool everybody else was using.

7.blurb: Nippur was Sumer's religious capital — home to the god Enlil's great temple, the place every Sumerian king had to be 'confirmed' by the priesthood to be considered legitimate. Harappan-style carnelian beads and cubical chert weights have turned up in Nippur's excavated levels, confirming the Meluhha trade reached deep into Mesopotamia's sacred interior, not just its coastal ports.

Sargon built the ancient world's first empire around 2334 BCE by conquering the Sumerian city-states and dragging their trade networks into a single system. His royal inscriptions specifically name three foreign lands whose ships tied up at his capital's docks: Dilmun, Magan, and Meluhha. Meluhha, almost certainly, was the Indus — which makes Sargon's accountants the first people to put the Harappans into a readable text.

Dilmun (modern Bahrain) shows up in Mesopotamian texts as both a sacred land (the Sumerian 'pure land' where the gods lived) and the great middleman port of the Gulf — the neutral swap point where Meluhha's carnelian and cotton traded hands for Sumerian silver and barley. Sargon's and later Ur III accountants mention it constantly. Getting rich on entrepôt trade between two civilizations is a business model that started here.

Magan (roughly modern Oman) was the Gulf's great copper mine, and Mesopotamian texts from Sargon's reign onward name it alongside Dilmun and Meluhha as the three lands that fed the Akkadian economy's appetite for metal, stone, and luxury goods. The same Omani copper flowed into both Harappan bronze and Sumerian bronze — one mine, two civilizations.

When Leonard Woolley cracked open the Royal Tombs of Ur in the 1920s, he found Queen Puabi buried with golden helmets, lapis lazuli harps — and long carnelian beads etched with distinctive white lines using a technique invented in the Harappan workshops at Chanhu-daro and Lothal. The Harappans were, in effect, jewelers to the Sumerian royalty.

Cuneiform, born at Uruk around 3200 BCE, is the reason the Harappan trade network has any named partners at all — every mention of Meluhha, Dilmun, and Magan comes from Sumerian and Akkadian tablets. If the Mesopotamians had written on palm leaves the way later Indians would, we'd have no idea the Indus trade network ever existed.

7.matchText: Mesopotamian texts mention cloth from Meluhha
7.blurb: Ur III administrative tablets (~2100 BCE) — the world's most detailed early bureaucratic archive — include entries for 'Meluhha cloth' among imported goods, alongside carnelian, ivory, and hardwoods. The tablets don't describe the cloth, but the date and context make it the earliest textual reference to cotton fabric anywhere in the world.

7.matchText: Wheeled vehicles originated in Mesopotamia around 3500 BCE
7.blurb: The earliest wheeled vehicles show up around Uruk in the late 4th millennium BCE — first as heavy sledge-wagons for hauling stone and grain, then, by the Early Dynastic period, as the famous four-wheeled Sumerian battle carts pulled by onagers. The technology then leaked outward along trade routes, reaching the Harappans in time to become toys for their children.

The Bronze Age maritime trade network that connected Meluhha to Magan and Dilmun had its African extension too: Kerma in Upper Nubia was plugged into the incense routes of the Red Sea and the Horn of Africa, trading ebony, ivory, leopard skins, and its own distinctive tulip-beaker pottery along chains that reached back into the same broader gift-diplomacy economy the Harappans used.

7.matchText: a web of maritime and overland routes
7.blurb: The missing leg of the trade network was the Elamite overland route. Iranian caravans operating from Susa and Anshan moved tin, lapis lazuli, and carnelian across the Zagros passes into Mesopotamia, running in parallel with the Harappan maritime route via Dilmun. Harappan-style seals and etched carnelian beads have been excavated at Susa itself, direct evidence that the Indus and Elam were in regular contact through the Iranian middleman zone. Elam was the trading partner on the west side of Iran; the Harappans were the suppliers on the east.

Elam's position on the western edge of the Iranian plateau made it the natural overland terminus for Harappan trade goods headed for Mesopotamia. Carnelian beads of the etched Harappan type have turned up at Susa, and some of the Harappan-style cubical stone weights have been found in Elamite contexts. The Iranian caravans carrying tin and lapis across the Zagros were moving the same commodity mix that the maritime Harappan ships were carrying via Dilmun — Elam and the Indus were the two non-Mesopotamian halves of the same Bronze Age luxury network.

7.matchText: silver and gold, which were relatively scarce in the Indus region
7.blurb: The highland silver and gold the Harappans imported came across the Iranian plateau through the Elamite caravan network, moving through trading stations at Susa and Anshan before being handed off to Mesopotamian or Harappan buyers. Elam's entire Bronze Age wealth model was built on being the middleman who taxed every pack-animal crossing the Zagros — every shekel of silver reaching an Indus workshop was paying a toll to an Elamite scribe on the way.

9.blurb: Sargon's Akkadian Empire ran for about 180 years before collapsing around 2154 BCE under a combination of internal revolt, Gutian raids from the Zagros mountains, and — we now know — the same 4.2 kiloyear drought event that was hammering the Indus and the Yangtze at exactly the same moment. The Akkadian collapse is one of the earliest documented cases of climate helping to take down an empire.

9.matchText: fall of the Third Dynasty of Ur in 2004 BCE
9.blurb: The Third Dynasty of Ur was Mesopotamia's great Sumerian revival — a tightly run bureaucratic empire under Ur-Nammu and Shulgi that produced the first law code and the last burst of Sumerian-language literature. Its collapse in 2004 BCE, partly from Elamite invasion and partly from the same climate crisis hitting the Harappans, ended the Meluhha trade almost overnight. The Harappan export markets dried up with the Gulf tablets.

9.matchText: Liangzhu civilization of the Yangtze delta collapsed
9.blurb: Liangzhu — China's hydraulic-engineering rice state, the one with the 100-square-kilometer flood-control network and the jade-filled elite tombs — went under on the leading edge of the same 4.2ky climate event around 2400–2300 BCE. Catastrophic flooding swamped the embankments and the whole civilization disappeared under layers of sediment; nobody found it again until the 20th century.

9.matchText: catastrophic outburst flood at Jishi Gorge
9.blurb: In 1920 BCE a massive landslide dammed the upper Yellow River at Jishi Gorge; when the dam failed, it released a flood estimated at 300,000–500,000 cubic meters per second — one of the largest freshwater floods of the Holocene. A 2016 Science paper argued this is the real event behind the Great Flood legend and the founding of the Xia Dynasty by Yu the Great. It hits the same climate window that was emptying out the Harappan cities.

The trade collapse lines up almost exactly with the 2004 BCE sack of Ur, when the Elamite Shimashki king Kindattu led an army west, burned Ur, captured the last Ur III king Ibbi-Sin, and dragged him back to Anshan in chains. The end of the Ur III state broke the Mesopotamian demand side of the Meluhha pipeline — and the Harappan export cities that had sustained themselves on that pipeline started thinning out inside the same generation. The Elamites pulled the trigger; the Indus caught the recoil.

9.matchText: Mesopotamian trade that the Harappans had sustained for over four centuries
9.blurb: Through the four hundred years of peak Indus–Mesopotamia trade, Elamite middlemen at Susa and Anshan had been the other major player in the triangle, taxing caravans and operating warehouses along the overland route. When the Gulf trade withered after 2000 BCE, Elam's Grand Regent (sukkalmah) dynasty pivoted away from the fading Harappan network toward a diplomatic-heavyweight game with Babylon and Mari — the same sukkalmah period when Hammurabi of Babylon was addressing the Elamite king as 'father' in the Mari letters.

10.matchText: Achaemenid Empire that would later conquer Mesopotamia
10.blurb: In 539 BCE, Cyrus the Great of Persia marched into Babylon and ended three thousand years of independent Mesopotamian kingship — the Achaemenid Persians spoke a close cousin of Sanskrit and worshipped gods with names recognizable to the Rigveda. The same Indo-Iranian language family that filtered into the Indus plains in 1500 BCE eventually conquered the other end of the old Bronze Age world too.

The Mitanni (~1500–1300 BCE) ruled a kingdom across northern Mesopotamia and Syria, and their royal dynasty had Indo-European personal names and swore oaths by Mitra, Varuna, and Indra — the exact gods worshipped in the Rigveda. They were Bronze Age contemporaries of the Kassite dynasty in Babylon, and they're the smoking gun that the same Indo-Iranian migration that reached the Punjab also reached the Fertile Crescent.

10.matchText: China, at the other end of Bronze Age Asia
10.blurb: China's story is the mirror image of the Indus: continuous cultural memory carried by a single tradition that never forgot itself. The Xia Dynasty, Yu the Great, the Yellow Emperor, the jade dragons of Hongshan — all of these stayed alive in Chinese texts and ritual for four thousand years and are still invoked today. The Harappans got archaeologically forgotten; China's Bronze Age got archaeologically confirmed by its own surviving memory.
